our_argument_summary: Our review verdicts WIN-011 as 'Not Demonstrated' because the Mohe gravity anomaly is unconfirmed: Membach SG found 0.0 uGal, China SG network found 0.0 uGal, and the coupling constant 1.67 nT/uGal is built on this unconfirmed data.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py computes kappa_obs = 10.9 / 6.5 = 1.677 nT/μGal, then compares this to the hardcoded constant KAPPA = 1.67. The 10.9 nT comes from BOU 2017 eclipse magnetic anomaly and 6.5 μGal from Mohe 1997. Both numerator and denominator are hardcoded literals in the source code, not fetched from any data API.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Everything is hardcoded. KAPPA = 1.67 is a constant. The 'observed' value 10.9/6.5 is a ratio of two hardcoded numbers. The 5% tolerance check is trivially passed because all three values (KAPPA, 10.9, 6.5) were defined together. No data is fetched for this domain.
data_sources_used: None — all values are hardcoded literals in monitor.py
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: 5% tolerance on the coupling constant comparison, but irrelevant since both 'predicted' and 'observed' are hardcoded from the same source.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No significant discrepancies. Our review correctly identifies the circular nature of the coupling constant derivation. However, our review does not explicitly state that the monitor.py code uses hardcoded 6.5 rather than fetching any gravimeter data.

holes_found.description: Our detail_claim states the dome model claims '+15.7 μGal' at Mohe, but the dome's own raw text (02-wins.txt) says '-6.5 uGal' and the dome's code (monitor.py) uses 6.5 in its coupling calculation. The value 15.7 does not appear anywhere in the dome model's site, raw text, or code. This appears to be an error in our review — we are misrepresenting the dome's claim.
holes_found.recommendation: Correct detail_claim to reflect the actual dome claim of ~6.5 μGal (or -6.5 uGal per the dome's raw text). The mismatch between +15.7 and -6.5 is a factual error that a dome defender could use to discredit our review.

holes_found.description: Our detail_claim places Mohe in 'Tibet'. Mohe is actually in Heilongjiang Province, far northeast China — it is the northernmost city in China, approximately 3,000 km from Tibet. This geographic error undermines credibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Correct 'Mohe (Tibet)' to 'Mohe (Heilongjiang Province, northeast China)' in the detail_claim.

holes_found.description: Our review says 'Membach SG detected 0.0 uGal' which oversimplifies. The Van Camp et al. (2001) study using four superconducting gravimeters during the 1999 eclipse found 'no identifiable eclipse-related change above ambient noise' and set an upper bound of γ < 10⁻¹⁶ m²/kg on gravitational shielding. Saying '0.0 uGal' implies impossible precision. The correct statement is that no signal was detected above the instrument noise floor.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase to 'no eclipse-related gravity signal above ambient noise' rather than '0.0 uGal'. Consider citing the actual upper bound.

holes_found.description: Our review does not distinguish between the Wang et al. (2000) Physical Review D paper and the Yang & Wang (2002) Astrophysics and Space Science follow-up paper. The 2000 paper reports 6-7 μGal anomalies at first and last contacts, while the 2002 paper reanalyzed the data. The dome model appears to cite the 6.5 value, which aligns with the 2000 paper.
holes_found.recommendation: Specify which paper is being cited. The 2002 follow-up reportedly showed 'much weaker evidence of anomalies than their first 1997 study.'

stronger_arguments.description: Wang et al. (2000) explicitly state 'no significant anomaly during the very solar eclipse was found.' The anomalous gravity valleys of 6-7 μGal occurred at FIRST CONTACT and LAST CONTACT, not at eclipse maximum. This is critical because gravitational shielding would peak at maximum eclipse (maximum coverage), not at the contacts. The dome model claims -6.5 μGal as if it were an eclipse-maximum effect, but the paper describes a contact-timing effect that is inconsistent with any shielding mechanism.
sources: Wang et al. (2000) Physical Review D 62, 041101(R)
Yang & Wang (2002) Astrophysics and Space Science 282, 245-253

stronger_arguments.description: The coupling constant κ = 10.9/6.5 = 1.677 nT/μGal is derived from exactly TWO data points: one magnetic measurement (BOU 2017) and one gravity measurement (Mohe 1997), from different eclipses, different continents, different instruments, and different years separated by two decades. A ratio of two single measurements is not a 'constant' — it is a coincidence. Any two nonzero measurements yield a ratio. The dome model presents this as a fundamental coupling constant without any independent validation, error propagation, or theoretical derivation from first principles.

The Allais effect (anomalous gravity during eclipses) has been investigated for over 60 years with inconsistent results. Yang & Van Flandern concluded 'no unambiguous detections within the past 30 years when consciousness of the importance of controls was more widespread.' The scientific consensus is that the effect, if real, is below the noise floor of current instruments. Building a coupling constant on an unconfirmed, contested measurement from a single eclipse makes the entire WIN-011/012 chain unreliable.
sources: Van Camp et al. (2001) Journal of the Geodetic Society of Japan 47(1), 387-392

kernel_of_truth: Eclipse gravity research is a legitimate scientific field. Wang et al. published in Physical Review D — a prestigious, peer-reviewed journal. The Mohe measurements were conducted with a calibrated LaCoste-Romberg gravimeter and the data was carefully processed. The observation of ~7 μGal anomalies at eclipse contacts is genuine data that has been discussed in the scientific literature for over 25 years. The dome model correctly identifies that this is an unexplained observation in mainstream physics. Where the dome model goes wrong is in (a) treating an unconfirmed, contested measurement as established fact, (b) misrepresenting the timing (contacts vs. maximum), (c) ignoring the extensive null results from superior instruments, and (d) using this single contested data point to derive a 'fundamental coupling constant.'
deeper_analysis:
WIN-011 claims that a 1997 gravimeter reading at Mohe shows an eclipse gravity anomaly that confirms the dome model's electromagnetic-gravitational coupling. Our review correctly verdicts this as 'Not Demonstrated,' and the underlying logic is sound — the Mohe result is indeed unconfirmed by superior instruments. However, our review contains two factual errors that need correction.

First, our detail_claim states the dome model claims '+15.7 μGal,' but the dome's own raw text says '-6.5 uGal' and the monitor.py code uses 6.5 in its coupling calculation (kappa_obs = 10.9 / 6.5). The value 15.7 does not appear anywhere in the dome model. Second, we place Mohe in 'Tibet' when it is actually in Heilongjiang Province, northeast China — roughly 3,000 km from Tibet. These errors are the kind of thing a dome defender could seize on to discredit our entire review.

The stronger version of our argument should emphasize what Wang et al. (2000) actually found versus what the dome model claims they found. The paper explicitly states 'no significant anomaly during the very solar eclipse was found.' The 6-7 μGal anomalous 'valleys' occurred at first contact and last contact — NOT at eclipse maximum. This timing is incompatible with gravitational shielding, which would maximize when the Moon's disc covers the most area between Sun and Earth (i.e., at maximum eclipse). The dome model's use of -6.5 μGal as if it were a maximum-eclipse measurement is a misrepresentation of the source paper.

The monitor.py code analysis reveals a deeper problem: Domain 7 (EM-Gravity Coupling κ) computes kappa_obs = 10.9 / 6.5, then compares this to KAPPA = 1.67 with a 5% tolerance. All three values — KAPPA, 10.9, and 6.5 — are hardcoded constants defined in the same source file. No data is fetched. The 'validation' is comparing a hardcoded constant to a ratio of two other hardcoded constants that were chosen specifically to produce that ratio. This is pure circular reasoning encoded in software, creating the appearance of computational validation where none exists.

Finally, our review could be strengthened by noting that the ratio of any two nonzero measurements yields a number. Calling that number a 'coupling constant' requires (a) independent theoretical derivation predicting the value, (b) independent experimental confirmation across multiple events, and (c) demonstrated predictive power for new observations. The dome model's κ = 1.67 nT/μGal satisfies none of these criteria. It is a post-hoc ratio of two cherry-picked numbers from two different eclipses, validated only against itself.
